figures.caption: Figure 1: Drag prediction for the cylinder flow. a) An instantaneous visualization of cylinder wake at Re = 4000. The yellow and blue isopleths represent the vortex criterion Q = ±1U2/D2, respectively. The streamwise velocity is shown by the contour plot, where the color ranges from blue for low speeds, through white (corresponding to the free stream speed, U = 1), to yellow for high speeds. The first two best-performing locations are marked with red and orange symbols. Circle, diamond, asterisk, cross, and square symbols denote the results at Reynolds numbers from 2000 to 4000, respectively (details in Methods). The inset is the zoomed-in view of the first best-performing locations. The first two best-performing locations align with the key physical characteristic of the cylinder wake: they are symmetrically positioned 45◦ (relative to the X-axis) upstream of the separation points (≈ 90◦)18,24, corresponding to the most informative locations in cylinder wake dynamics. b) Performance approaches the ceiling value R2 b exponentially with increasing Ni. R2 b is estimated by the fit R2 = R2 b −C1 exp(C2Ni), where C1 and C2 are parameters of linear regression on the semi-logarithmic axes. Symbols represent the mean values averaged over five predictions. c) Prediction (tp = 1D/U ) versus DNS time series of Cd in the testing set at Re = 4000 (prediction: blue line; DNS: black line). The R2 metric reached 0.887±1.4×10−3 (mean ± standard deviation over five independent predictions).

Figure 2: The exponential scaling law. Error bars represent the mean ± standard deviation from five independent predictions. Solid lines are fitted by Eq. 3. The inset shows the dependence between the standard deviation σ, the timescale τ , and Reynolds number Re. Error bars on τ/σ2 denote the 99% confidence interval, where the uncertainty in σ is estimated by subsampling with a subsample size of Ndata∆ts, and the uncertainty in τ is estimated from the exponential fit. Here, Ndata and ∆ts represent the number of data points and data sampling interval (see Methods), respectively. The gray line in the insert is a linear fit to τ/σ2, with a regression R2 fit = 0.96.

Figure 3: The exponential growth (linear mechanism) and linear growth (nonlinear mechanism) of error. a) Temporal evolution of perturbation energy ∆Ek (U2D3) in semi-logarithmic coordinates. The inset shows the Lyapunov exponent λ, obtained from the slope of ln(∆Ek) in the exponential growth regime, with the red line indicating a linear regression. Data in the panel a and the inset correspond to different initial perturbations: solid lines (panel a) and black symbols (inset) for u′ = (0, 0, 10−7N ), where N is the standard Gaussian operator; dash-dotted lines and blue squares for u′ = (0, 10−5v0, 0); the dotted line and yellow plus sign for u′ = (0, 0, 10−5w0). The dashed line represents a finite-amplitude perturbation with energy ||u′||2 > 10−2. Horizontal gray lines demarcate four growth regimes: initial modulation (∆Ek < 10−8), exponential growth (10−8 < ∆Ek < 10−1), transition (10−1 < ∆Ek < 100, and linear growth (100 < ∆Ek). b) Temporal evolution of perturbation energy ∆Ek in linear-linear coordinates. Arrows in this figure mark the two growth regimes: the solid arrow denotes exponential growth, while the dashed arrow indicates linear growth.

Figure 4: The inverse-cascade of error. a) Temporal evolution of the error spectra at Re = 4000: energy spectrum of the Y -dependent perturbation velocity w′ = w − w0 on the X = 2D plane (X = 1D, 3D also examined for robust validation), averaged along the Z-direction and 10 independent DNS realizations. In exponential growth (purple lines), the spectra begin at t = 0.4D/U with a time interval of 0.8D/U ; in linear growth (orange lines), they begin at t = 4.8D/U with the same interval. This temporal evolution corresponds to that of ∆Ek(t) in Fig. 3a. The black line represents E∆(k, t)/E(k) = η = 0.8 for numerically estimating ks (η = 0.9 has also been checked for robustness). The Kolmogorov energy spectra for u, v, and w on the X = 2D plane are shown in the inset as blue, red, and black lines, respectively, ensemble-averaged along the Z-direction over 750 velocity fields from 10 independent DNS realizations. b) Temporal evolution of critical wavenumber kc at moderate Reynolds numbers. The function kc(t) are scaled by ka = kc(ti + 2.8), where ti is the initial time when kc becomes detectable once reaching ηE(k). The black dash-dotted line denotes the theoretical scaling result kc(t) = αε−1/2t−3/2.
